Pasumpon Thevar

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Pasumpon Thevar

### Associate of Netaji Subash Chandra Bose

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Oct 30, 2024

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

Share

Ukkirapandi Muthuramalingam Thevar aka Pasumpon Thevar, close associate of Netaji, who fought to get the Criminal Tribes Act repealed, and played a major role in spreading INA down South.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hR6e!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F31f72614-12b9-41e6-aa13-6fa109b6ac4d_599x900.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hR6e!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F31f72614-12b9-41e6-aa13-6fa109b6ac4d_599x900.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Of the many discriminatory laws that the British enacted, during their reign, one of the most notorious ones was the Criminal Tribes Act of 1871.

While the British framed the act as a response to banditry and Thuggee cults, it quickly morphed into a punitive measure against entire communities suspected of supporting the 1857 uprising—especially nomadic and artisan tribes with decentralized lifestyles that clashed with imperial control.

The act branded entire ethnic groups as *hereditarily criminal*, meaning people—often children—were born into criminality regardless of their actual actions. This obliterated the notion of individual justice and cemented generational trauma.

Tribes were forced to register, carry passes, and were often confined to settlements where they had to report to authorities daily. Many lost the freedom to move, trade, or practice traditional livelihoods.By criminalizing nomadic life and indigenous occupations, the Act also threatened cultural identities, oral traditions, and social cohesion. It targeted not just people, but their way of life.

In Madras Presidency, this act was widely used in the Southern part, primarily Madurai , Tirunelveli and Ramnad, where communities like Thevars, Maravars were bought under this draconian act. One man however decided to rebel against this unjust act, Pasumpon Thevar.

He was born in the village of Pasumpon in Ramanthapuram district, to Ukkirapandi Thevar and Indiraniammal on Oct 30, 1908. He grew up under the care of his maternal grandmother. after his mother passed away when he was small. He was mentored by family friend Kuzhanthaisami Pillai, who arranged his schooling too. He however had to drop out of studies, due to a plague epidemic and an ongoing legal case, over inheritance.

Pasumpon Muthuramalinga Thevar didn’t just oppose an unjust law—he channeled his fury into action that rattled the colonial machinery in South India. When 19 Maravar villages near Appanad were forced into CTA registration, Thevar didn’t flinch. He mobilized mass defiance, urging people to reject this bureaucratic branding, even if it risked persecution.

The British response was classic colonial obstinance—they broadened the Act instead of repealing it. But Thevar countered with sharper, louder, and more widespread resistance. His campaign framed CTA not merely as a bad law but as a symbol of ethnic and caste-based oppression. He exposed the racial hierarchy embedded in colonial legislation and how it unfairly targeted southern communities, especially the Maravars and Thevars.

Thevar didn’t just lead rallies—he stirred conscience. His oratory, layered with cultural pride and political urgency, reframed southern identity from “suspect” to “sacred.”

The Justice Party’s indifference to the plight of marginalized communities under the Criminal Tribes Act cemented a deep divide. For Thevar, their elitist leanings and lack of grassroots empathy stood in direct contrast to his lived reality and moral vision.

Joining the Indian National Congress wasn’t simply strategic—it was born from a desire for solidarity and national muscle in his anti-CTA movement. His charisma infused new energy into the Congress in the south, especially among disenfranchised communities seeking representation.

His speeches weren’t mere rhetoric—they carried the weight of suppressed voices and the fire of historical injustice. From Ramanathapuram to Tirunelveli they rallied not just for Congress—but for Thevar’s vision of dignity and cultural pride.

However when he wanted to contest for President of Ramnad District Board, some Congress leaders led by Satyamurthy opposed him. His aspiration to lead locally was not just about political prestige—it was about reasserting dignity for communities systematically excluded. Congress’s pushback highlighted internal caste biases, making his entry into **Congress Socialist Party** a move toward more inclusive ideals.

Thevar channeled his frustration into action—organizing **Thevar and Mukulathor youths** across southern Tamil Nadu. His strategy fused identity with purpose, turning youth into instruments of cultural and electoral revival against the Justice Party’s elitism.

That the Justice Party tried to bar him from traveling beyond Ramanathapuram speaks volumes. His oratory and magnetic influence were potent enough to threaten entrenched powers. Limiting his movement was a last-ditch colonial tactic to contain an idea that was already free.

He however had the last laugh, defeating the Raja of Ramnad, who was the Justice Party candidate, by a landslide, that showed how deeply the masses connected with his voice. It was not just a vote—it was a referendum on oppression.

The irony of Congress forming the government but failing to repeal the CTA—despite Thevar’s tireless advocacy—underscored internal class divisions and political inertia. For Thevar, it must’ve felt like betrayal dressed as progress.

His founding of the *Pasumalai Mahalakshmi Workers Union* and the prolonged strike at Madura Knitting Company revealed another facet of his leadership: solidarity with the working class. That seven-month imprisonment was a badge of defiance, proof that he didn’t just speak for the oppressed—he stood alongside them.

One of Netaji’s closest associates, Pasumpon supported him during the 1939 Tripura Congress President election. Gandhiji put up his candidate, B.P.Sitaramaiah, for post of Congress President, but Netaji won the election hands down. His efforts to secure votes for Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose across the southern states were decisive. His influence among Mukulathors, Thevars, and Congress grassroots workers gave Netaji a vital edge.

Netaji’s victory over Gandhiji’s nominee B.P. Sitaramaiah wasn’t just surprising—it marked a seismic ideological shift, briefly giving voice to more radical anti-imperial strategies. Thevar was an engine behind that tremor. Thevar admired Bose’s uncompromising stand against British rule and resonated with his militant nationalism. Their bond wasn’t just political—it was forged in shared defiance and belief in total liberation.

  
After Netaji was pressured to resign, the Congress's centrist ideology no longer aligned with Thevar’s vision for India. So on **June 22, 1939**, he joined Bose in launching the **Forward Bloc**, aiming to galvanize youth, workers, and disenfranchised communities into revolutionary action.

Thevar’s disappointment with Congress’s inaction on the Criminal Tribes Act had crystallized into purposeful rebellion. His decision to host a massive rally for Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose in **Madurai on September 6, 1939** wasn’t just an act of admiration—it was a declaration.

  
The rally was Thevar’s way of aligning himself with a more uncompromising vision for India’s independence—one that didn’t tolerate injustice in the name of political pragmatism. The rally galvanized southern communities around Netaji’s ideals and implicitly challenged Congress’s caste-inflected apathy. The Criminal Tribes Act was still untouched, and the gathering reminded everyone who had truly stood up for their dignity.

  
Madurai—a city steeped in Tamil pride and revolutionary memory—became a backdrop to this electrifying moment. The crowd wasn’t just there for Netaji. They were there because **Thevar had called**, and his word had weight.

Thevar shaped not only Tamil Nadu’s political conscience—but also its emotional allegiance to Subhas Chandra Bose and the INA. When Netaji envisioned the Indian National Army as a bold arm of resistance, Thevar became his heartbeat in the south—organizing rallies, building support networks, and inspiring youth to volunteer, especially in districts like Ramnad, Madurai, and Tirunelveli.

His charisma turned recruitment into a cultural mission. For many Southern Tamil youths, joining the INA wasn't just a patriotic act—it was an expression of their ancestral pride, especially with leaders like Thevar casting it in the language of honor and duty.

Even today, the reverence for Netaji and the INA across southern Tamil Nadu owes much to Thevar’s passionate groundwork. The street names, statues, and annual commemorations bear witness to a flame that hasn’t dimmed

Thevar’s support for Dalit temple entry reforms wasn’t just a bold social stand—it was a spiritual realignment. Leading temple entry campaigns placed him at the crossroads of tradition and progress, showing that cultural pride need not exclude reform.

Winning Mudukalathur in 1946 wasn’t just about votes—it symbolized mass validation of decades of activism. From CTA resistance to labor rights, his work resonated across caste and class lines.

The CTA too was repeased in 1946, his relentless battle against colonial stigma finally saw justice. The very communities once branded as "hereditary criminals" could now begin healing.

When Congress expelled dissenters, Thevar’s emergence as Forward Bloc President wasn't reactionary—it was poetic. It solidified his identity as a fighter who couldn't be contained by party lines or political orthodoxy.

While Nehru's Congress swept most of the country in 1952, Thevar won both **Aruppukottai Lok Sabha seat** and the **Mudukulathur Assembly seat**, showing that **ideological fidelity and mass respect** triumphed over party branding in southern Tamil Nadu.

  
When Congress fell short of a majority in the state assembly, Thevar tried to forge a **non-Congress coalition** with Left parties—an early attempt to build an alternative to centrist dominance. Though it didn’t succeed, it revealed his strategic foresight and willingness to unite diverse ideological streams for Tamil Nadu's autonomy and justice.

  
Winning both seats again in 1957 reaffirmed that Thevar wasn’t a flash of charisma—he was **a sustained force**, whose dedication to denotified tribes, laborers, and cultural pride continued to resonate across election cycles.

However there was a split in the Forward Bloc itself, with the section headed by Sheel Bhadra Yajee and Mohan Singh, merging with the Congress.

Remaining with the undiluted Forward Bloc, Thevar underscored his commitment to alternative politics—where grassroots justice mattered more than party optics. His decision to enter the Lok Sabha was not just a shift in strategy but a continuation of his battle from a national platform.

The Maravars, closely aligned with Thevar and the Forward Bloc, saw his leadership as a reclamation of dignity. Meanwhile, the Pallars, historically marginalized, viewed Congress as a gateway to empowerment and social reform. Sasivarna Thevar of Forward Block, won the poll, and the victory celebrations triggered off caste clashes between the Maravars and Pallars, which soon spread like wildfire.

Returning from Delhi, Pasumpon took part in a Peace Conference on September 10, 1957 along with Sasivarna Thevar and Velu Kudumban, a Pallar, that managed to broker a peace between the warring communities.

His arrest in connection with the **murder of Congress leader Emmanuel Sekaran** deepened tensions—but his **acquittal** served as a judicial moment of clarity amid a politically charged environment. Through it all, Thevar never lost the trust of his core supporters, and his image as a fighter for social justice remained intact.

After release, Thevar built an impressive **coalition of Forward Bloc and Left parties**, channeling years of grassroots activism into electoral strategy. This led to **Congress's first defeat in Madurai**, a stronghold, making it a landmark moment in Tamil Nadu’s post-independence political history.

However his health began to deteriorate and four years later he passed away on Oct 30, 1963, yes the same date on which he was born.

His worldview was never materialistic. He envisioned freedom not just as political autonomy but as a **cultural and spiritual awakening**—infusing nationalism with devotion, discipline, and Dharma.

At a time when Congress was seen as synonymous with nationalism, Thevar offered an alternative: one that resisted elitism, stood by the working class, and honored **indigenous values and traditional ethos**.

His belief system echoed **Indic ideals of duty, sacrifice, and collective identity**—not divisiveness. Whether defending temple entry or organizing labor strikes, Thevar saw justice as **a sacred obligation**, not a political tool.

He was proudly Tamil, yet never insular. His alliance with Netaji Bose, embrace of Forward Bloc, and INA mobilization show that **Thevar’s soil was Tamil Nadu—but his sky was India.**

While the Forward Bloc held Leftist appeal, Thevar **opposed Marxist atheism and class-only analysis**. The Soviet model, which negated spiritual traditions, clashed with his view of societal harmony rooted in **spiritual order and ancestral reverence**.

The Left’s **disavowal of Netaji Bose**, whose spiritual nationalism Thevar admired, created an irreparable schism. Moreover, their **hostility toward Hindu cultural frameworks** put Thevar at odds with Marxist rhetoric.

He was critical of Dravidian ideology,condemning their **secessionist ideas**, viewing Tamil identity as an integral part of Indian civilization, not separate from it. Thevar **stood against linguistic chauvinism** that fractured unity and questioned the **denigration of indigenous spiritual practices** by certain Dravidian ideologues.

He didn’t just carry Netaji’s message southward—he **infused it with Tamil fire**, turning spiritual nationalism into a lived experience for thousands. Whether mobilizing INA volunteers, resisting caste oppression, or challenging political orthodoxy, Thevar never compromised his vision of **Bharat as a sacred civilizational entity**.

My article on him here
